title: I agree with your perspective.
subtitle: However, in practice, many monoliths evolve into tangled, unstructured "spaghetti code" over time. In such cases, breaking the system into…
excerpt: However, in practice, many monoliths evolve into tangled, unstructured "spaghetti code" over time. In such cases, breaking the system into…

content.html: I agree with your perspective. If you have a clean, well-structured, modular, and thoroughly tested monolith, then sticking with a modular monolith is often the better choice—it avoids the operational complexity, network latency, and debugging challenges that come with microservices.

However, in practice, many monoliths evolve into tangled, unstructured "spaghetti code" over time. In such cases, breaking the system into microservices can be a pragmatic way to address both technical debt and team scalability. As you rightly pointed out, microservices are often more about people than technology—especially when teams with different skill sets (or even preferred programming languages) need to work independently.

In my own experience, the main pain point was having everything—ETL pipelines, internal API calls (via curl), SSO, and business logic—all crammed into a single repository. This made development, testing, and deployment extremely slow and error-prone. Separating these concerns into distinct, independently deployable services significantly improved both developer velocity and system reliability.
